issues_found: The record does not include the source's explicit disclaimer that 'The views expressed in this post are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the position of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York or the Federal Reserve System.' While not a factual error, this is relevant context for interpreting the source's authority.

The topic field is set to 'macro catalysts' which is reasonable, though the notes correctly identify that the source is academic commentary rather than a market-moving catalyst. This is acceptable but worth noting.

corrected_fields.why_it_matters: This regional survey provides timely evidence on business cost dynamics that inform inflation analysis. Rising health insurance and utilities costs—distinct from tariffs—suggest broad-based cost pressures beyond trade policy. The data implies potential margin compression for businesses and may influence Fed policy considerations around service sector inflation. Note: this is regional (NY-Northern NJ) rather than national data, and the authors explicitly state their views do not reflect the Federal Reserve's position.
corrected_fields.macro_context: Regional business cost data suggests inflation pressures broadened in 2025 beyond goods prices to include services components like healthcare and utilities. Health insurance costs rising faster than wages (12.9-14.2% vs 3.4%) indicates non-labor cost pass-through may be dampening wage growth (examined in follow-up post). Expected moderation in 2026 (to 5.4% services, 4.8% manufacturers) aligns with 2024 pace. The authors note that tariffs contributed to goods/materials cost increases for manufacturers but represent only part of the overall cost story.
